analogy: Think of "National Income Accounting" through the chapter's own opening images: Chapter 1 Introduction Introduction Introduction Introduction Introduction You must have already been introduced to a study of basic microeconomics. This chapter begins by giving you a simplified account of how macroeconomics differs from the microeconomics that you have known. Those of you who will choose... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 33333 Introduction, 55555 Introduction, 1.1 EMERGENCE OF MACROECONOMICS, 1.2 CONTEXT OF THE PRESENT BOOK OF MACROECONOMICS. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
National Income Accounting is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that chapter 1 Introduction Introduction Introduction Introduction Introduction You must have already been introduced to a study of basic microeconomics.

The text explains that this chapter begins by giving you a simplified account of how macroeconomics differs from the microeconomics that you have known.

The text explains that those of you who will choose later to specialise in economics, for your higher studies, will know about the more complex analyses that are used by economists to study macroeconomics today.

The text explains that but the basic questions of the study of macroeconomics would remain the same and you will find that these are actually the broad economic questions that concern all citizens – Will the prices as a whole rise or come down?

The text explains that is the employment condition of the country as a whole, or of some sectors of the economy, getting better or is it worsening?

The text explains that what would be reasonable indicators to show that the economy is better or worse?

The text explains that what steps, if any, can the State take, or the people ask for, in order to improve the state of the economy?

The text explains that these are the kind of questions that make us think about the health of the country’s economy as a whole.

The text explains that these questions are dealt within macroeconomics at different levels of complexity.

The text explains that in this book you will be introduced to some of the basic principles of macroeconomic analysis.

The text explains that the principles will be stated, as far as possible, in simple language.

The text explains that sometimes elementary algebra will be used in the treatment for introducing the reader to some rigour.

The text explains that if we observe the economy of a country as a whole it will appear that the output levels of all the goods and services in the economy have a tendency to move together.

The text explains that for example, if output of food grain is experiencing a growth, it is generally accompanied by a rise in the output level of industrial goods.

The text explains that within the category of industrial goods also output of different kinds of goods tend to rise or fall simultaneously.

The text explains that similarly, prices of different goods and services generally have a tendency to rise or fall simultaneously.

The text explains that if aggregate output level, price level, or employment level, in the different production units of an economy,

The text explains that 22222 Introductory Macroeconomics bear close relationship to each other then the task of analysing the entire economy becomes relatively easy.

The text explains that instead of dealing with the above mentioned variables at individual (disaggregated) levels, we can think of a single good as the representative of all the goods and services produced within the economy.

The text explains that similarly, the price or employment level of this representative good will reflect the general price and employment level of the economy.

The text explains that particularly, when these attributes start changing fast, like when prices are going up (in what is called an inflation), or employment and production levels are going down (heading for a depression), the general directions of the movements of these variables for all the individual commodities are usually of the same kind as are seen for the aggregates for the economy as a whole.

The text explains that we will see below why, sometimes, we also depart from this useful simplification when we realise that the country’s economy as a whole may best be seen as composed of distinct sectors.

The text explains that for certain purposes the interdependence of (or even rivalry between) two sectors of the economy (agriculture and industry, for example) or the relationships between sectors (like the household sector, the business sector and government in a democratic set-up) help us understand some things happening to the country’s economy much better, than by only looking at the economy as a whole.

The text explains that while moving away from different goods and focusing on a representative good may be convenient, in the process, we may be overlooking some vital distinctive characteristics of individual goods.

The text explains that for example, three general kinds of commodities may be taken as a representative of all commodities being produced within the economy: agricultural goods, industrial goods and services.

The text explains that for microeconomics the ‘macro’ (meaning ‘large’) phenomena affecting the economy as a whole, like inflation or unemployment, were either not mentioned or were taken as given.

The text explains that the nearest that microeconomics got to macroeconomics was when it looked at General Equilibrium, meaning the equilibrium of supply and demand in each market in the economy.

The text explains that macroeconomics tries to address situations facing the economy as a whole.

The text explains that thirdly, and most importantly, in a large number of situations society (or the State, or the people as a whole) had decided to pursue certain important social goals unselfishly (in areas like employment, administration, defence, education and health) for which some of the aggregate effects of the microeconomic decisions made by the individual economic agents needed to be modified.

The text explains that they may be entities like the government, corporation, banks which also take different economic decisions like how much to spend, what interest rate to charge on the credits, how much to tax, etc.

The text explains that however, in addition, it has to deal with policies aimed at also modifying these forces, if necessary, to follow choices made by society outside the markets.

The text explains that typically, each such body will have one or more public goals to pursue as defined by law or the Constitution of India itself.

The text explains that obviously they often have to go beyond economic objectives and try to direct the deployment of economic resources for such public needs as we have listed above.

The text explains that adam Smith is regarded as the founding father of modern economics (it was known as political economy at that time).

The text explains that we address ourselves, not to their humanity but to their self-love, and never talk to them of our own necessities but of their advantage’ is often cited as an advocacy for free market economy.

The text explains that the Physiocrats of France were prominent thinkers of political economy before Smith.

The text explains that these events made economists think about the functioning of the economy in a new way.

The text explains that the fact that the economy may have long lasting unemployment had to be theorised about and explained.

The text explains that keynes’ book was an attempt in this direction.

The text explains that unlike his predecessors, his approach was to examine the working of the economy in its entirety and examine the interdependence of the different sectors.

The text explains that we shall examine the working of the economy of a capitalist country in this book.

The text explains that to carry out production they also need natural resources – a part consumed in the process of production (e.g. raw materials) and a part fixed (e.g. plots of land).

The text explains that in short, a capitalist economy can be defined as an economy in which most of the economic activities have the following characteristics (a) there is private ownership of means of production (b) production takes place for selling the output in the market (c) there is sale and purchase of labour services at a price which is called the wage rate (the

The text explains that it also takes into account various interlinkages which may exist between the different sectors of an economy.

The text explains that this is what distinguishes it from microeconomics; which mostly examines the functioning of the particular sectors of the economy, assuming that the rest of the economy remains the same.

The text explains that macroeconomics emerged as a separate subject in the 1930s due to Keynes.

The text explains that the Great Depression, which dealt a blow to the economies of developed countries, had provided Keynes with the inspiration for his writings.

The text explains that in this book we shall mostly deal with the working of a capitalist economy.

The text explains that hence it may not be entirely able to capture the functioning of a developing country.

The text explains that macroeconomics sees an economy as a combination of four sectors, namely households, firms, government and external sector.

The text explains that what is the difference between microeconomics and macroeconomics?

The text explains that what are the important features of a capitalist economy?

Source: NCERT Class 12 Introductory Macroeconomics, Chapter 1: National Income Accounting
